exemplar.title: To what extent does meat consumption (kg per capita per year) of different types of meat (Beef, Pork, Poultry, Fish and Seafood) influence the prevalence of cardiovascular diseases in a globally representative sample of 50 countries

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does meat consumption (kg per capita per year) of different types of meat (Beef, Pork, Poultry, Fish and Seafood) influence the prevalence of cardiovascular diseases in a globally representative sample of 50 countries

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Wellâfocused, clearly articulated research question with appropriate scope for an EE
Comprehensive justification of data sources and choices, showing conscious selection aligned with the research question
Clear and detailed explanation of biological background, epidemiological context, and study scope
weaknesses: Minor imprecision in articulation of causal mechanisms limits full mastery
Some procedural details of data cleaning and sampling methodology are under-explained
Data presentation tables could be better integrated to facilitate immediate comparison
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a very clear and appropriately scoped research question, explains the rationale for source selection, and communicates the topic with detailed background and context. Small lapses in methodological transparency (e.g. incomplete data-cleaning steps) and occasional redundancy prevent a top mark, but overall performance sits solidly at the upper band, warranting 5/6.

strengths: Generally relevant and coherent application of scientific literature to support background and contextualise results
Correct and appropriate use of biological and statistical terminology throughout
Integration of source data into analysis sections shows sound subject knowledge
weaknesses: Predominantly descriptive synthesis of sources with limited critical appraisal of reliability and bias
Occasional inconsistencies and minor inaccuracies in units and technical definitions
Repetitive use of secondary infographics without deeper interrogation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student applies a strong range of reputable sources and uses terminology accurately, demonstrating solid knowledge. However, application remains largely descriptive, and critical engagement with source reliability is superficial. Performance aligns with the upper 3â4 band, meriting 4/6.

strengths: Identification of key confounders (dietary patterns, lifestyle factors) shows critical awareness of study limitations
Transparent use of skewness testing to justify choice of Spearmanâs correlation
Logical structure of analysis with clear links between results and the research question
weaknesses: Analysis remains predominantly descriptive, without exploration of causation or statistical significance levels
Discussion of confounders lacks quantitative assessment or concrete methodological improvements
Argument occasionally overstates correlation as causation and contains inconsistencies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work exhibits sound critical thinking: the student conducts appropriate statistical tests, identifies major study limitations and structures the argument logically. Nonetheless, the analysis stops short of deeper evaluation (e.g. p-values, confidence intervals) and sometimes conflates correlation with causation. These mixed strengths and weaknesses place the work at the top of the 4â6 band, meriting 6/12.

strengths: Clear overall structure with all expected EE components (title page, TOC, sections, appendices)
Consistent application of layout conventionsâpage numbers, headings, figure labelsâenhancing readability
Professional presentation of figures and tables integrated into the narrative
weaknesses: Inconsistent subsection numbering and occasional mis-numbered headings
Typographical errors and sentence fragments interrupt coherence
Figure captions and axis labels sometimes mismatch or omit units
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is tidy and follows the expected EE format, with effective use of headings, lists, tables and figures. Minor structural irregularities (mis-numbering), typographical lapses and caption inconsistencies prevent a flawless presentation. Performance aligns with the top band for presentation, warranting 4/4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The essay adheres to the expected EE structure with a title page, abstract-style introduction, table of contents, clearly numbered sections, analysis, evaluation, conclusion, references and appendices. Headings and sub-headings facilitate navigation and the flow is generally logical.

Nonetheless, minor structural issuesâmis-numbered subsections (e.g., repeated 1.3), occasional abrupt transitions, and an over-long literature review section relative to analysisâindicate that the structure, while clear, is not fully optimised.

These qualities satisfy the âclear and appropriate structureâ descriptor (3-4 band). Given the noted imperfections, a score of 3 is warranted.

The student includes a formatted title page with word count, a detailed table of contents, labelled figures and tables, and an extensive works-cited list in a recognisable citation style. Page numbers and section headings appear to be present, and overall presentation is neat and readable.

However, some formatting conventions are inconsistently applied: figure numbering duplicates, figure captions blend with descriptive text, and minor typographical errors persist. Citation formatting varies, and in-text references are not always consistent with the bibliography.

Given that most layout requirements are met but with noticeable lapses, the work fits the upper 1-2/3-4 band; awarding 3 reflects generally correct application with some shortcomings.

The essay follows a logical sequenceâintroduction, methodology, analysis, evaluation, conclusionâand the central argument (that different meat types show weak or negligible correlations with CVD prevalence) is stated and revisited. Supporting evidence from both the studentâs data and literature is presented.

However, the argumentâs coherence is weakened by inconsistencies: negative correlations are sometimes portrayed as evidence of protective effects without considering reverse causality or socio-economic factors; parts of the analysis contradict the biochemical rationale outlined earlier. These gaps reduce persuasiveness and indicate only partial coherence.

Accordingly, the strand meets the âadequate but inconsistent argumentâ criterion within the 4-6 band; a score of 4 is appropriate.

The title and opening paragraphs state a precise research topic and the student provides substantial background on cardiovascular disease, meat categories, heme iron and saturated fats. Key biological mechanisms (atherosclerosis, LDL oxidation, foam-cell formation) are explained with diagrams and literature support, demonstrating clear understanding of the topicâs context. 

Explanations are generally accurate and go beyond simple descriptionâfor example, the student links heme iron to LDL oxidation and plaque formation. Minor lapses in clarity (occasional syntax errors, uneven subsection numbering) slightly detract from overall effectiveness, but do not seriously hinder comprehension. 

Because communication is detailed, logically organised and largely accurate, the work meets the descriptor for the 5-6 band, though some imprecision and redundancy suggest the lower end (5) rather than full mastery (6).

The essay draws on an extensive bibliography of reputable scientific and statistical sources and integrates them when explaining biological mechanisms and comparing findings. Source data (OWID, FAO, IHME) are applied to generate correlation analyses, and supporting literature is cited to contextualise results.

However, much of the use remains descriptiveâstudies are quoted to affirm or dispute the studentâs findings, but synthesis is limited and sometimes repetitive. There is little critical appraisal of source reliability beyond generic statements, and some claims (e.g., heme iron values) rely heavily on secondary infographics without deeper interrogation.

Given the generally relevant but partly superficial application, the work satisfies the 3-4 band at its upper end; a score of 4 best reflects the competent yet not consistently effective integration of sources.

The student conducts basic statistical analyses (skewness tests and Spearman correlations) and interprets coefficients using an external classification table. Analysis of each meat type identifies direction and strength of correlations and attempts to link results to biochemical mechanisms.

However, the analysis remains largely descriptive; it reports correlation values without exploring potential causation, confounders or statistical significance levels (p-values). No confidence intervals or error discussion for correlations are provided, and the rationale for treating time-series observations as independent is not addressed. Thus relevance to the research question is clear but depth is limited.

These features correspond to âadequate analysis â¦ partially relevantâ within the 4-6 band; assigning a mid-level score of 4 recognises the effort while reflecting its modest sophistication.

The student selects sizeable, publicly available datasets (OWID, FAO, IHME) and explains the rationale for choosing themâglobal coverage, reliability and age-standardisation. The sampling strategy (50 countries stratified by GDP, six time points) is described, and statistical methods (skewness test leading to Spearmanâs rank correlations) are outlined and exemplified with spreadsheet screenshots.

These choices constitute an appropriate range of relevant secondary sources and demonstrate conscious selection decisions. However, the methodology is limited by lack of control for confounding variables and by using aggregated national data, issues the student recognises only later. Primary data collection or multivariate analysis is absent, and some procedural details (e.g., data cleaning steps) are vague.

Overall the approach is suitable and sufficiently explained to reach the 5-6 band, but the noted gaps and limited depth justify awarding 5 rather than 6.

The evaluation section acknowledges discrepancies between findings and existing literature, and identifies uncontrolled variables (dietary patterns, lifestyle factors, meat cut variations, data limitations). These reflections show some critical awareness of study weaknesses and data bias.

Yet the discussion often reiterates earlier content and stops short of rigorous evaluation; for example, it does not quantify how confounders might alter correlations, propose concrete methodological improvements, or integrate statistical uncertainty into conclusions. Conclusions are cautious but not fully substantiated by in-depth critique.

This positions the work firmly in the 4-6 band for discussion/evaluation, with a score of 4 given the partial but not consistently well-supported analysis.

The research questionââTo what extent does meat consumption (kg per capita per year) of different types of meat â¦ influence the prevalence of cardiovascular diseases in a globally representative sample of 50 countries?ââis explicitly stated, focused on specific variables (five meat types, CVD prevalence) and limited to a defined dataset (50 countries, 1994-2019). 

The introduction justifies the relevance of the question by linking dietary factors to global CVD mortality and identifying knowledge gaps in meat-specific impacts. The scope (secondary data, correlation approach) is appropriate for a Biology EE and is neither too broad nor too narrow for the 4,000-word limit. 

While the question is clear and focused, it could articulate the biological mechanismâepidemiological link more explicitly and acknowledge causal limitations. Nonetheless, it aligns well with the 5-6 band; a score of 5 is assigned to recognise minor weaknesses in precision and focus.

Throughout the essay the student employs appropriate biological and statistical terminologyâe.g., "atherosclerosis", "foam cells", "Spearmanâs rank correlation", "age-standardised prevalence"âand defines key biochemical terms such as LDL and oxysterols. These terms are generally used correctly, demonstrating sound subject knowledge.

Nevertheless, occasional inaccuracies and awkward phrasing appear (e.g., conflating LDL particles with LDL cholesterol, inconsistent unit notation, minor grammatical slips) and some technical explanations lack precision. Such lapses detract from consistency and accuracy.

Consequently, the performance aligns with the 3-4 band, leaning toward the upper mark (4) for generally sound but not flawless terminology and conceptual use.

annotations.comment: Table 1 correctly applies skewness testing to assess distribution normality, supporting transparent decision-making on choice of Spearmanâs correlation.
The discussion on poultry consumption correctly cites literature but omits statistical context (e.g., variation across countries), reducing depth of critical evaluation.
The example scatter plot in Figure 6 is redundant and distracts from main results. Focusing on the key combined trendlines would improve coherence and streamline the analysis section.
Figure 1 is well-captioned and integrated into the narrative, aiding the explanation of atherosclerosis through clear visual support.
The sample meat consumption table omits corresponding CVD prevalence side by side, limiting immediate comparison. Including both datasets in one table would facilitate direct analysis.
An incomplete sentence appears at the end of the stroke description. Ensuring each paragraph is fully contained prevents reader confusion and maintains professional presentation.
The introduction effectively cites studies linking meat components to CVD risk, integrating source material to contextualise the research question and demonstrating sound subject knowledge.
The biochemical pathway describing LDL oxidation and foam cell formation is comprehensively explained and supported by multiple scholarly sources, indicating effective application of source material.
The rationale for selecting OWID, FAO, and IHME datasets is clearly explained, showing conscious source selection aligned with the research question and methodological appropriateness.

The research question is clearly articulated, specifying variables (meat types, CVD prevalence) and dataset scope (50 countries). This focus supports a well-defined investigation and aligns with the criteria for a clear, appropriately scoped research question.
Subsection numbering in the table of contents is inconsistent (1.3 repeated), which disrupts navigation. Correct sequential numbering would improve structural clarity and coherence.
The inclusion of the word count at the start meets the EE formatting requirement, demonstrating adherence to word-limit conventions.
The opening paragraph contains a sentence fragment (âWhich often leadsâ¦â). Revising for grammatical completeness will enhance readability and professionalism.
The detailed explanation of stroke subtypes demonstrates accurate and consistent use of medical terminology, contributing to clear subject understanding.
Unit notation is inconsistent (â4.75 g per 75 gramsâ). Standardising units (e.g., âmg per 75 gâ) will improve precision and technical accuracy.
The section on saturated fats is cut off mid-discussion. Completing the explanation of the LDL receptor mechanism will ensure consistency and clarity.
Figure 3âs axes are described but the units (mg/75 g) are not labelled directly on the axes. Including units on each axis improves interpretability.
The CVD prevalence table spans columns unevenly and breaks across pages, affecting readability. Consistent column widths and page breaks would improve formatting.
Skewness test results are shown without discussion of how non-normality thresholds were chosen. Justifying why the 2ÃSE comparison dictates test choice would deepen the analysis.
The sampling description omits how the random selection was implemented. Detailing randomisation procedures and inclusion criteria will strengthen methodological transparency.
Spearmanâs correlation coefficients are presented without accompanying significance levels (p-values) or confidence intervals, limiting interpretation of statistical robustness.
The general evaluation adeptly identifies uncontrolled dietary and lifestyle confounders, demonstrating critical awareness of study limitations and their impact on validity.
The conclusion effectively reiterates the research question and synthesises findings, linking back to the studyâs scope and highlighting implications for public health recommendations.
The graph title and axes descriptions in the caption do not match the axis labels in the figure. Ensuring consistency between caption text and axis labeling is essential.

Interpreting a negative Spearmanâs Ï as suggesting that increased beef consumption ââreducesââ disease risk overstates correlation as causation. Recognise observational limits and potential reverse causality.
The evaluation of beef data notes disagreement with literature but does not quantify how confounders or meat cut variation might alter the correlation, leaving conclusions under-substantiated.
The pork evaluation acknowledges external contradictions but fails to propose specific methodological adjustments (e.g., stratifying by pork cut) to resolve discrepancies.
